does: This paper addresses two core challenges in agentic reinforcement learning for tool-using language models. It proposes Progressive Reward Shaping (PRS), a curriculum-inspired approach that breaks the binary reward signal into multiple intermediate milestones—first rewarding correct tool call formatting, then factual correctness, and finally answer quality. Complementarily, it introduces Value-based Sampling Policy Optimization (VSPO) to mitigate gradient degradation in Group Relative Policy Optimization when trajectories within a rollout group receive identical rewards. The paper demonstrates these techniques on tool-augmented reasoning tasks, showing improvements in both convergence speed and sample efficiency for LLM-based agents navigating long-horizon planning problems.
connects: This work builds on the growing intersection of agentic reinforcement learning and large language models, extending prior research on tool-integrated reasoning systems and curriculum learning in RL. It directly addresses limitations of recent agentic RL frameworks that optimize over full tool-interaction trajectories but suffer from sparse reward signals—a classic problem in RL that curriculum learning has tackled before. The paper responds to specific technical issues with GRPO-based optimization algorithms used in LLM fine-tuning, where gradient noise from identical advantages reduces learning efficiency. It connects to prior work on reward shaping for complex sequential decision-making and value-based policy optimization methods.
enables: This approach enables more efficient training of autonomous agent systems built on LLMs, potentially broadening the applicability of agentic RL to more complex reasoning domains where sparse rewards have previously limited scalability. By decomposing the reward signal into learnable sub-tasks, the framework could support hierarchical learning of multi-step tool use, opening pathways toward agents that progressively master reasoning capabilities. The value-based sampling strategy may generalize beyond tool-use contexts to other domains with grouped rollout optimization. Future work could extend PRS to more complex task structures, explore adaptive milestone design, and investigate whether these techniques transfer to longer horizons, broader tool sets, and more diverse reasoning tasks in real-world applications.
